California
Fair Political
Practices Comrnission
December 24′  ■990
WiIIian E. Ne\^/berry
crand Jury
County Government
San Luis obispo,
Dear Mr. NewberrY:
This is in response to your letter dated September 24, 1990
pertaining to grand iurors and their obligations under the
iolitical 
-Reforn Act: 1
center
CA  93408
Re:
Your Request fOr Adv■
ce
Our File No. I-90-629
CONCLUS IONS
juries are locaI government agencies and therefore are
certain minimum disclosure requirements of the
Reform Act.
jurors are not public officials within the meanj'ng of
Tf,erefore, grand jurors cannot be lreated-as triaf
OUESTIONS
1. Are there any circumstances under which a grand juror is
not required to file a conflict of interest form 730? If so, what
are these circumstances?
2. Should grand jurors be treated as trial jurors?
3. 
Do grand iurors fa}l, within the definition of a
'rdesignated enpl,oyeerr?
Grand

the Act.

1 co.r"rrr."nt code Sections 81ooo-91015. All' statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated. 
cornrnission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations. Section
l-8000, et seql- AtI references to regul-ations are to TitLe 2,
oivision 6 of the California code of Regulations'
428 J Street, Suite 80o o P.O. Box 8o7 o Sacramento CA 95804-0807 . (916)322-5660


our File No. A-90-629
Page 2
It is reasonably foreseeable that grand jury reports and
recommendations concerning government agencies and decisions on
contracts could affect private financial interests. 
These
investigative and contracting porrers are clearly not merely
advisory functions. Therefore, grand jurors do falL within the
def inition of 'tdesignated enpl-oyee. rt
FACTS
Your letter states that you are currentJ-y revising your Grand
Jury }landbook and are concerned with the treatment of grand jurors
and their conflict of interest filing obligations. 
You also
inquire about the treatment of grand jurors as compared to trial
jurors. 
AdditionalLy, you inquire about the inclusion of grand
Jurors in the definition of "designated employee.r' Your belief is
that grand jurors only investigate and reconmend and do not nake
decisions.
ANALYSIS
Chapter 727 amended Section 82048 to read as follows:
I'Public official" 
means every member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency, but does not include judges and court
commissioners in the judicial branch of government'
ItPublic officialrt also does not include mertbers of the
Board of Governors and designated enployees of the state
Bar of catifornia, 
rnembers of the Judicial council, and
nembers of the Commission on Judicial Performance.. '.
Regulation 18700 provides a definition of the term rrMemberrr
as f oll,ows:
ttMenber'r sha1l include, but not be limi.ted to,
salaried or unsalaried nembers of boards or commissions
with decision-making authority. 
A board or cornmission
possesses decision-making authority whenever:
(A) It may make a final governmental decisionr'
(B) It may conpel a governmental. decision; or it
may prevent a governmental decision either by
reason of an excfusive power to initiate 
the
decision or by reason of a veto which may-not be
overr j,dden ; or
(c) It makes substantive recommendations which
are, and over an extended period of time have been,
regularly a!:proved without significant amendment or
rnodification by another public official 
or
governmental agencY.


our FiIe No. A-9o-529
Paqe 3
In the Whittinqton Advice Letter, No. A-85-185 (copy
enclosed) , we advised that:
In addition to presenting crirninal indictments, grand
juries are empowered to examine the operations of local
governnents and to make reports and recommendations. See,
generally, Penal- code Section 925, et seo. In additionr
grand juries nay contract for the services of experts. Pena1
Code Section 926. The charge of the grand jury is broad,
and, in any given year and county, the grand jury by
necessity will focus its attention on certain specific
matters. Although the perceived effect of grand jury reports
and recornmendations seens to be mixed, it is clear that the
statutes confer governmental powers on the grand juries. 
ft
is our conclusion that grand juries are agencies subject to
the Political Refom Act. we also think that it is
reasonably foreseeable that grand jury reports and
recommendations concerning government agencies and decisions
on contracts could affect private financial- interests. 
These
i,nvestigative and contracting polrers are clearly not rnerely
advisory functions. 
See comnission of cal. State Gov. orq. &
Econ. v. Fair PoIitical Practices commission, 75 caL.App.3d
716 (L977).
Subsequently, in the Botz Advice Letter, No. A-87-083 (copy
enclosed), the conmission advised that grand jurors hrouLd be
subject to certain rninj-rnurn discl,osure requj,rements. Accordingly,
grand jurors are subject to certain rninimurn disclosure
requj.rements and must file a statement of economic interests
(described by you as rra conflict of interest form 730'r).
Unlike trial jurors, grand jurors have broad po$/ers as
described in the Whittinqton Advice Letter, suDra. Unlike trial
jurors who are selected for a particular case, grand jurors
usually have povrers beyond any particul-ar case. Accordingly,
grand jurors are not treated as trial jurors for purposes of the
Act.
Your tetter asks whether grand jurors fa1l within the
definition of trdesignated employee.t' The tern "designated
employeetr is defined in Section 82019. That definition also has
certain explicit exclusions, to wit:
trDesignated employeerr does not include .: . any
unsalaried menber of any board or conmission which
serves only an advisory function....
Grand jurors are deerned to be designated in the conflict of
interest code pursuani-to Section 87302. Further, as stated in
the Gua1co Advice Letter dated JuIy 23 | L986 (copy enclosed) , the
Commission has concluded that rrgrand jurors do, in fact, have an
impact upon governmental decisions and are not purely advisory in
nature.rr Accordingly, since they are not excluded as serving a
purel-y advisory function, they faII within the definition of the
terrn "designated employee. "


Our Fil,e No. A-90-62 9
Page 4
crand jury conft j,ct-of-interest codes must require disclosure
of economic interests with ties to those governmental agencies
which it wilt investigate if the decisions of the grand jury may
foreseeably have a rnaterial financial effect on these economic
interests. 
This determination should be made at the local 1eve1 .
The only issue over which the Commission has authority is the
extent of financial discl,osure required frorn grand jurors.
The Commission has suggested model disclosure language which
it indicated would be considered adequate rninirnum disclosure. 
The
suggested language is:
A11 investrnents and business positions in business
entities, and income from sources which have done
business with the county in the previous two years and
income from individuals who are ernpLoyees of the county
and all interests in real property.
For those counties lrhose grand juries engage in a broader review,
broader disclosure categories would be appropriate.
If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to
contact me.
Very truly yours,
Scott Hallabrin
Acti/rng General counsel
&r"^P^*'t-
By: Lynda Doherty \
Staff Services Analyst
Legal Division
:ld
Encl osures
